title: Beef Wellington
intro: A center-cut fillet wrapped in mushroom duxelles, prosciutto and golden puff pastry. This showstopper carves into rosy slices with a crisp, buttery crust.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the fillet generously, then sear in hot oil on all sides until deeply browned and immediately brush all over with mustard.
Blitz the mushrooms and fry in a dry pan until the moisture evaporates into a dry paste, then cool completely.
Lay overlapping prosciutto on cling film, spread the duxelles over it, and set the fillet on top.
Roll the film tightly to form a firm cylinder and chill for 20 minutes to hold its shape.
Unwrap onto rolled puff pastry, seal the edges, brush with egg yolk and score lightly.
Bake at 200 C for about 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the center reaches 52 C for medium-rare.
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing into thick portions.
tip: Chill the wrapped log twice so the pastry stays crisp and the meat cooks evenly.

title: Boeuf Bourguignon
intro: Tender beef braised slowly in red Burgundy with bacon, pearl onions and mushrooms. This rustic French classic rewards patience with a deep, silky sauce.
ingredients: 1.4 kg beef chuck, cut into large cubes
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Fry the lardons until crisp, remove, then brown the seasoned beef in the rendered fat in batches.
Soften the chopped onion, carrot and garlic, then stir in tomato paste and dust with flour.
Return the beef and bacon, pour in the wine and stock, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and braise in a 160 C oven for about two and a half hours until fork-tender.
Meanwhile brown the pearl onions and mushrooms separately in butter until glossy.
Fold the onions and mushrooms into the stew and simmer for 15 more minutes.
Skim any fat, adjust the seasoning, and serve over mashed potatoes.
tip: Use a wine you would actually drink; a poor bottle leaves the sauce thin and sour.

title: Perfect Ribeye Steak
intro: A thick ribeye basted in foaming butter with thyme and garlic until it forms a mahogany crust. Restaurant-quality steak built on patience and constant basting.
steps: Pat the steaks dry, season heavily with salt, and let them sit at room temperature for 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a heavy skillet until it just begins to smoke.
Lay the steaks away from you and sear undisturbed for three minutes until a crust forms.
Flip, add butter, thyme and garlic, and tilt the pan to spoon the foaming butter over the meat.
Continue basting for three to four minutes until the center reaches 52 C for medium-rare.
Rest on a rack for five minutes, then finish with flaky salt and cracked pepper.
tip: A dry surface is the secret to browning, so never salt and sear a wet steak.

title: Chimichurri Skirt Steak
intro: Charred skirt steak sliced against the grain and drenched in bright, herby chimichurri. Smoky, garlicky and vivid with fresh parsley and red wine vinegar.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Stir together parsley, oregano, garlic, olive oil, vinegar and pepper flakes, then season and set aside.
Pat the steak dry, rub with neutral oil, and season aggressively with salt and pepper.
Heat a grill or cast-iron pan until very hot.
Sear the steak for about three minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest the meat for five minutes to keep the juices in.
Slice thinly against the grain and spoon plenty of chimichurri over the top.
tip: Let the chimichurri sit at least 15 minutes so the garlic and herbs mellow together.

title: Classic Cheeseburger
intro: Juicy smash-style patties with crisp lacy edges and melted American cheese. Piled into a toasted bun with all the diner-style trimmings.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Divide the beef into four loose balls without overworking the meat.
Butter and toast the cut sides of the buns until golden.
Press each ball hard onto a screaming-hot griddle to form a thin patty with craggy edges.
Season, cook two minutes until the edges crisp, then flip and top with cheese.
Cook one more minute until the cheese melts, adding a splash of water under a lid to help it along.
Build each burger on a toasted bun with onion, pickles, lettuce and tomato.
tip: Do not press the patty after flipping or you will squeeze out the juices.

title: Beef Bolognese Ragu
intro: A slow-simmered ragu of beef, soffritto and tomato enriched with a splash of milk. Deeply savory and perfect clinging to fresh tagliatelle.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Fry the pancetta until its fat renders, then add the onion, carrot, celery and garlic and soften slowly.
Raise the heat, add the beef, and brown well while breaking up any clumps.
Stir in the tomato paste, then pour in the wine and let it reduce by half.
Add the chopped tomatoes, milk and stock, then bring to a bare simmer.
Cook uncovered on low heat for at least two hours, stirring now and then.
Season to taste once the sauce is thick and glossy.
Toss with cooked tagliatelle and a little pasta water to coat.
tip: The milk tenderizes the meat and rounds out the acidity, so do not skip it.

title: Steak au Poivre
intro: Peppercorn-crusted filet mignon in a silky cognac and cream pan sauce. A bistro classic that feels effortlessly elegant.
steps: Press the crushed peppercorns firmly into both sides of the salted steaks.
Sear in hot oil and butter for about three minutes per side for medium-rare, then rest on a plate.
Soften the shallot in the same pan, then remove from the heat and add the cognac.
Return to the heat carefully to flame off the alcohol, then pour in the stock.
Reduce by half, stir in the cream, and simmer until the sauce coats a spoon.
Return any resting juices to the sauce and spoon it generously over the steaks.
tip: Crack the peppercorns coarsely so they crackle in the crust rather than turning to dust.

title: Slow-Braised Short Ribs
intro: Meaty short ribs braised in red wine until they surrender off the bone. The glossy reduction turns into a luxurious, spoonable sauce.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the ribs well and sear in hot oil until browned on every side, then set aside.
Soften the onion, carrot, celery and garlic in the same pot until lightly colored.
Stir in the tomato paste, then deglaze with the wine and reduce by half.
Return the ribs, add the stock and rosemary, and bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise in a 150 C oven for about two and a half hours until tender.
Lift out the ribs and strain the sauce, then reduce it on the stove until glossy.
Spoon the sauce over the ribs to serve.
tip: Make it a day ahead and lift off the solidified fat for a cleaner, richer sauce.

title: Triple-Cooked Sunday Roast Beef
intro: A slow-roasted rib of beef cooked low for an even blush, then blasted for a burnished crust. Served with a deep, savory pan gravy.
steps: Salt the roast heavily and leave it uncovered in the fridge overnight to dry the surface.
Sear all sides in hot oil until browned, then set it on the onions and thyme in a roasting tray.
Roast at 120 C until the center reaches 50 C, roughly one and a half hours.
Rest the meat loosely tented while the oven heats to 240 C.
Return the roast for about ten minutes to crisp and darken the exterior.
Rest again, then make gravy by whisking flour into the pan juices with wine and stock.
Carve thick slices and pour the strained gravy over the top.
tip: Use a probe thermometer, as the low temperature makes visual doneness cues unreliable.

title: Korean Beef Bulgogi
intro: Thin ribeye marinated in soy, pear and sesame, then seared hot and fast. Sweet, savory and smoky, it is a Korean barbecue favorite.
steps: Whisk the pear, soy, sugar, sesame oil, garlic and ginger into a marinade.
Toss the sliced beef in the marinade and chill for at least 30 minutes.
Heat neutral oil in a wide pan or griddle until very hot.
Sear the beef in a single layer in batches so it caramelizes rather than steams.
Cook each batch two to three minutes until browned at the edges.
Scatter with spring onions and sesame seeds and serve with steamed rice.
tip: Freeze the ribeye for 30 minutes before slicing to get paper-thin pieces.

title: Texas-Style Beef Chili
intro: A no-beans chili of cubed chuck simmered in a rich dried-chile sauce. Bold, smoky and thickened by the beef itself as it breaks down.
ingredients: 1.3 kg beef chuck, cut into small cubes
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the beef and brown it in hot oil in batches, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic in the same pot until fragrant.
Stir in the chili powder, cumin and paprika and toast for one minute.
Return the beef, add the tomatoes and stock, and bring to a simmer.
Cover partially and cook on low for about two hours until the beef is tender.
Whisk the masa harina with a little water and stir it in to thicken the chili.
Simmer ten more minutes, then season to taste and serve.
tip: Toasting the spices before adding liquid deepens their flavor dramatically.

title: Beef Stroganoff
intro: Strips of tender beef and mushrooms in a tangy sour cream and mustard sauce. A quick, comforting classic served over buttered noodles.
ingredients: 600 g beef sirloin, cut into strips
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the beef strips, then sear quickly in hot oil until browned and remove.
Melt the butter and soften the onion and mushrooms until golden.
Stir in the mustard and paprika and cook for one minute.
Pour in the stock and simmer to reduce slightly.
Lower the heat and stir in the sour cream without letting it boil.
Return the beef with any juices and warm through for two minutes.
Serve immediately over buttered noodles or rice.
tip: Add the sour cream off a rolling boil so the sauce stays smooth and does not split.
